class_schedule.topic: What Is Personality? Major Approaches and Assessment
learning_objectives: Define personality and explain why individual differences matter in psychology
Compare the six major approaches to personality: trait, biological, psychodynamic, humanistic, cognitive, and social-learning
Describe common personality assessment methods and their psychometric properties

lecture_notes.introduction: Personality psychology asks the most fundamental question about human nature: why are people different from one another, and how do those differences matter?
key_concepts: Personality as stable patterns of thoughts, feelings, and behaviors
Nomothetic (general laws) vs. idiographic (unique individual) approaches
Self-report inventories, projective tests, behavioral observation
Reliability and validity of personality measures
The person-situation debate (Mischel vs. trait theorists)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students complete a brief Big Five inventory and discuss the experience of being measured

review.value: Mischel argued personality traits weakly predict behavior; trait theorists countered with aggregation and strong situations arguments
Must balance efficiency (self-report) with validity concerns (social desirability, acquiescence bias)

class_schedule.topic: Trait Theory and the Big Five
learning_objectives: Trace the development of trait theory from Allport to the Big Five
Describe the five-factor model: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism
Evaluate the cross-cultural universality and predictive validity of the Big Five

lecture_notes.introduction: Trait theory provides the dominant framework in modern personality psychology, offering a taxonomy of individual differences that predicts important life outcomes.
key_concepts: Allport's lexical hypothesis: important traits are encoded in language
Factor analysis and the statistical extraction of traits
OCEAN: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism
Facets within each factor (e.g., assertiveness and warmth within extraversion)
Big Five predicts job performance, relationship quality, health, and longevity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Group exercise: predict which Big Five dimensions best predict success in different occupations (surgeon, teacher, entrepreneur)

review.value: The strongest personality predictor of job performance across virtually all occupations; also predicts health behaviors and longevity
Personality traits important to human interaction will be encoded as single words in natural language; basis for factor-analytic trait discovery

learning_objectives: Explain the heritability of personality traits based on twin and adoption studies
Describe Eysenck's biological theory of extraversion-introversion
Discuss the role of neurotransmitters, hormones, and brain structures in personality

lecture_notes.introduction: Biological approaches to personality examine the genetic, neurological, and physiological bases of individual differences.
key_concepts: Behavioral genetics: twin studies show ~50% heritability for Big Five traits
Shared vs. non-shared environmental influences
Eysenck's arousal theory: introverts have higher baseline cortical arousal
Gray's reinforcement sensitivity theory: BAS (approach) and BIS (inhibition)
Testosterone and dominance; cortisol and stress reactivity; serotonin and mood regulation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze published twin study data to calculate concordance rates and estimate heritability

review.value: Proportion of variance in a trait attributable to genetic differences; does NOT mean a trait is determined by genes or unmodifiable
Behavioral Inhibition System underlies anxiety/avoidance; Behavioral Activation System underlies reward-seeking and positive emotion

learning_objectives: Describe Freud's structural model (id, ego, superego) and psychosexual stages
Evaluate modern psychodynamic concepts: attachment, object relations, and defense mechanisms
Compare Rogers's person-centered theory with Maslow's hierarchy of needs

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychodynamic and humanistic theories offer contrasting views of human nature: one emphasizes unconscious conflict, the other emphasizes growth and self-actualization.
key_concepts: Freud: id (pleasure principle), ego (reality principle), superego (moral standards)
Defense mechanisms: repression, projection, rationalization, sublimation
Object relations theory: internal working models of relationships
Rogers: unconditional positive regard, conditions of worth, real vs. ideal self
Maslow: hierarchy of needs and self-actualization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Defense mechanism identification exercise: categorize defenses from case descriptions
setup_requirements: Defense mechanism handout with definitions and examples

review.value: Rogers: acceptance and support regardless of behavior; necessary for congruence between real and ideal self
Early relationships with caregivers create internal mental representations (objects) that shape all later relationships

learning_objectives: Explain Bandura's social-cognitive theory including self-efficacy and reciprocal determinism
Describe Mischel's cognitive-affective processing system (CAPS) model
Discuss Dweck's implicit theories of personality (fixed vs. growth mindset)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cognitive and social-learning approaches emphasize that personality arises from how people interpret situations, learn from experience, and regulate their own behavior.
key_concepts: Bandura's reciprocal determinism: person, behavior, environment interact bidirectionally
Self-efficacy: domain-specific confidence in one's ability to perform
Mischel's CAPS: if...then... behavioral signatures across situations
Rotter's locus of control: internal vs. external
Dweck: entity (fixed) vs. incremental (growth) theories of ability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Self-efficacy survey: students rate self-efficacy across academic, social, and physical domains, then discuss domain specificity

review.value: Belief in one's ability to succeed in specific situations; strongest predictor of behavioral persistence and achievement
Personality consists of stable if...then patterns: IF in a social situation, THEN the person responds with anxiety; provides cross-situational consistency within situation types

learning_objectives: Evaluate how personality predicts health, relationships, career success, and well-being
Describe personality development and change across the lifespan
Discuss cultural and gender differences in personality

lecture_notes.introduction: Personality traits are not merely abstract descriptions; they predict real-world outcomes across the lifespan with meaningful effect sizes.
key_concepts: Conscientiousness predicts longevity, job performance, and academic success
Neuroticism predicts relationship dissatisfaction and mental health vulnerability
Mean-level personality changes across the lifespan (maturity principle)
Rank-order stability increases through young adulthood
Cross-cultural personality structure: Big Five emerge across cultures with some variation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data analysis exercise: students examine personality-outcome correlations from a published meta-analysis

review.value: People tend to become more agreeable, conscientious, and emotionally stable with age, peaking in middle adulthood
Conscientiousness is the strongest Big Five predictor of longevity; neuroticism predicts health complaints but not necessarily disease

questions.question: A twin study finds that identical twins raised apart have a personality trait correlation of r = .49, while fraternal twins raised together have a correlation of r = .23. Estimate heritability and explain why the non-shared environment is more important than the shared environment for personality.
questions.answer: Heritability is approximately 2 x (rMZ - rDZ) = 2 x (.49 - .23) = .52, or 52%. Shared environment can be estimated as rMZ - heritability = .49 - .52, which is essentially zero. This means that growing up in the same household does not make siblings more similar in personality. Non-shared environment (unique experiences, different peer groups, different treatment by parents) accounts for the remaining ~48% of variance. This pattern is one of the most robust findings in behavioral genetics.

questions.question: According to Gray's reinforcement sensitivity theory, how would a person with a dominant BAS (behavioral activation system) differ in behavior and personality from a person with a dominant BIS (behavioral inhibition system)?
questions.answer: A person with a dominant BAS would be more sensitive to rewards and positive stimuli, showing greater approach motivation, impulsivity, and positive emotionality. They would correspond to high extraversion and sensation-seeking. A person with a dominant BIS would be more sensitive to punishment, novelty, and threat cues, showing greater avoidance, caution, and negative emotionality. They would correspond to high neuroticism and anxiety-proneness. The BAS is linked to dopaminergic pathways, while the BIS involves serotonergic and septohippocampal systems.

rubric.key: Accurately describes BAS-dominant personality and behavior
Accurately describes BIS-dominant personality and behavior
Links to neural systems or Big Five traits

questions.question: Compare Rogers's concept of conditions of worth with Bandura's concept of self-efficacy. How does each explain maladaptive behavior, and what are the treatment implications?
questions.answer: Rogers: conditions of worth are the requirements others impose for acceptance. When self-worth depends on meeting others' standards, a person develops incongruence between their real self and ideal self, leading to anxiety and defensiveness. Treatment: provide unconditional positive regard in person-centered therapy to restore congruence. Bandura: self-efficacy is domain-specific confidence. Low self-efficacy leads to avoidance, giving up easily, and anxiety when facing challenges. Treatment: build self-efficacy through mastery experiences, modeling, verbal persuasion, and managing physiological arousal.

rubric.key: Accurately explains conditions of worth and implications

questions.question: Explain Mischel's cognitive-affective processing system (CAPS) model and describe how it resolves the person-situation debate.
questions.answer: The CAPS model proposes that personality consists of a network of cognitive-affective units (encodings, expectations, goals, values, self-regulatory plans) that are activated by specific situational features. This produces stable if...then behavioral signatures: a person may be anxious in social situations but calm in academic settings. The model resolves the person-situation debate by showing that personality is not about behaving the same way in every situation (cross-situational consistency) but about behaving consistently within the same types of situations (within-situation consistency). The stable pattern of variation across situations IS personality.

rubric.key: Shows how this resolves the person-situation debate

major_assignments.title: Personality Theory Integration Paper
major_assignments.description: Choose a well-known public figure (historical or contemporary) and analyze their personality from at least three theoretical perspectives (e.g., trait, psychodynamic, social-cognitive). Compare and contrast the perspectives, evaluating which provides the most compelling account.
objectives: Apply multiple personality theories to a single individual
Evaluate the strengths and limitations of each theoretical perspective
Synthesize competing explanations into a coherent personality portrait
Support theoretical claims with evidence from the person's life
deliverables: Application of at least three personality theories
Evaluation of each theory's explanatory power

criteria: Accurate application of at least three theories
Goes beyond surface-level description to deep analysis
Sophisticated application of three or more personality theories
Insightful integration and evaluation of competing explanations
Strong evidentiary support from biographical and research sources

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of personality theories and research findings

participation_and_discussion.description: Active engagement in class discussions and personality assessment exercises

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering trait, biological, and psychodynamic approaches

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all personality perspectives

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Personality assessment activities require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Extensions granted with advance notice and documentation.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of personality theories is encouraged. All written work must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Fabricating personality analyses will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to university LMS, personality assessment tools, and library databases.
